title: On Sidon Sets
speaker: Gilles Pisier
abstract: Analysis and Beyond - Celebrating Jean Bourgain's Work and Impact

proof_stages.label: Classical Sidon notions and equivalences
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up Sidon, randomly Sidon, and subGaussian subsets of the dual of the torus or a compact abelian group, and recalls the classical Rudin-Rider-Pisier equivalence together with Drury's union theorem.

proof_stages.label: Quasi-independent characterization and finite-union problem
proof_stages.summary: Quasi-independent sets are introduced as the arithmetic/combinatorial core of Sidon sets, yielding the proportional quasi-independent subset characterization and the open problem of whether every Sidon set is a finite union of quasi-independent sets.

proof_stages.label: Abstract orthonormal-system reformulation
proof_stages.summary: The classical notions are reformulated for uniformly bounded orthonormal systems, tensor-k Sidon systems are defined, the classical implications are shown to fail in this generality, and the Bourgain-Lewko theorem subGaussian implies tensor-five Sidon is stated.

proof_stages.label: Biorthogonal tensor-two theorem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states the sharpened theorem: two uniformly bounded systems biorthogonal to subGaussian systems have a tensor product system dominating l1, giving the optimal tensor-two Sidon conclusion.

proof_stages.label: Gaussian domination and tensor decomposition
proof_stages.summary: A Talagrand/Hahn-Banach domination theorem linearizes subGaussian systems as images of standard Gaussians, and projective/injective tensor norms reduce the key decomposition to the Gaussian Mehler-kernel case.

proof_stages.label: Extrapolation proof of tensor-two Sidonicity
proof_stages.summary: The proof pairs a sign-corrected tensor decomposition with the target polynomial, uses biorthogonality and the projective/injective estimates, then chooses the error parameter small enough to absorb the l1 coefficient sum.

proof_stages.label: Randomly Sidon versus tensor Sidon for systems
proof_stages.summary: Slepian comparison and Gaussian tensorization show that random tensor-k Sidonicity is the same as random Sidonicity, leading to an equivalence with tensor-k Sidonicity for k at least four under bounded biorthogonal assumptions.

proof_stages.label: Noncommutative and matrix-valued extension
proof_stages.summary: The theory is extended from scalar systems to irreducible representations and random matrix-valued systems, with analogous tensor-two and random tensor-k results and consequences for matrix convexity and singleton Sidon representations.

examples.name: Hadamard lacunary sequence 2^n
examples.note: The sequence 2^n is given as the classical concrete Sidon set, while also warning that it has extra special properties not shared by generic Sidon sets.

examples.name: Sidon system on half a probability space with a bad complement
examples.note: Putting a Sidon system on one half of a measure space and arbitrary bad functions on the other visibly shows why the implication Sidon implies subGaussian fails for abstract systems.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker constructs a system by putting a Sidon part on half the probability space and a bad part on the other half, then reasons why the l-infinity inequality preserves Sidonicity while sub-Gaussianity can fail.

examples.name: Products of bounded Hadamard-like sign matrices
examples.note: Matrices with entries of modulus 1/sqrt(n) and bounded operator norm illustrate the matrix-valued tensor-two Sidon theorem through a trace-class convex-hull consequence.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the tensor-two Sidon result to bounded sign/Hadamard-like matrices and derives that products of two such matrices give an equivalent absolutely convex hull for the matrix ball/trace-class norm.

passes.reason: The products are built from flat equal-modulus Hadamard-like sign matrices and serve as an application of tensor-two Sidonicity.
